items.q: Why does food provenance matter to consumers?
items.correct: It affects environmental impact, quality, ethics, and supports local economies.
items.random_wrong: It tells you the calorie content.
items.why: Students reduce food choice to price. Provenance (origin) raises questions of food miles, fair trade, animal welfare, seasonal availability, and supporting local producers - all consumer concerns.

items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.q: Are local foods always more sustainable than imported ones?
items.correct: Not always - production methods can outweigh transport emissions.
items.misconception: Yes - shorter distance always means lower emissions.
items.random_wrong: No - imported food is always better.
items.why: Students apply 'shorter is better' too simply. A heated UK greenhouse tomato may have higher emissions than a Spanish field tomato trucked north. Lifecycle analysis matters more than miles alone.

items.q: What is the temperature 'danger zone' for bacterial growth?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5-63°C. “0-5°C” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Salmonella. “Lactobacillus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many principles make up a HACCP system?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 principles. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
